concerns.text: The spec assumes 'dislocation density' can be quantified via 'texture analysis metrics' on 512×512 optical/SEM microscopy images. In materials science, dislocation density is a sub-micron feature typically requiring Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) or X-ray diffraction (XRD) line-broadening analysis. Optical/SEM 'texture' (grain orientation) does not directly measure dislocation density. Using texture metrics as a proxy for dislocation density without a validated physical model or TEM ground truth renders the predictor scientifically invalid and the resulting correlation spurious.

The validation target (R² > 0.7) is set against 'held-out test data' derived from the same observational dataset used for training. In materials fatigue, public datasets often contain multiple specimens from the same alloy batch or heat treatment. If the split is random (row-wise) rather than grouped by 'alloy batch' or 'heat treatment', the model will learn batch-specific noise rather than microstructural physics, leading to data leakage and artificially inflated R². The spec lacks a requirement for 'grouped' or 'stratified' cross-validation by alloy source, making the validation target non-independent of the training distribution.

Applying standard ANOVA to test the significance of microstructural features on fatigue life assumes linear relationships and homoscedasticity. Fatigue life (cycles) is typically log-normally distributed, and the relationship with microstructure is often non-linear (e.g., Hall-Petch). Using ANOVA on raw cycle counts without log-transformation or non-parametric alternatives violates statistical assumptions, rendering the p-values (FR-008) and significance claims (SC-004) methodologically unsound.
